title: The Role of Blankets and Comfort Objects During Psychedelic Sessions
body:
The Role of Blankets and Comfort Objects During Psychedelic Sessions

1. Opening Hook - That Moment You Reach for a Blanket

I’ll never forget the first time I tripped deep on psilocybin mushrooms without a blanket within arm’s reach. The room was chilly, the air felt thin, and after a few intense waves of emotion, I started to shiver uncontrollably. At that moment, all I wanted was something soft, something warm, something that felt like a hug. But I’d left the comfort objects out of the room, thinking they were just “nice-to-haves,” not necessities. It was a mistake. That gap - the absence of physical warmth and tactile safety - threw me off balance when I was most vulnerable. It made me realize how much these simple objects act like anchors in an otherwise turbulent experience.

Have you ever wondered why therapists and guides often suggest bringing a favorite blanket, a stuffed animal, or a comfort object to a psychedelic session? Is it just about staying warm, or is there something deeper going on? As someone who’s sat through dozens of ceremonies and therapeutic sessions over the past 15 years, I can tell you that blankets and comfort objects aren’t mere props or accessories. They’re vital tools that quietly support the mind and body to surrender and explore.

Think about it. Under the influence of psychedelics, our usual filters and buffers dissolve. Our sense of self, time, and space can feel unmoored. This can be exhilarating or terrifying, depending on how safe and grounded we feel. When your body senses safety through a weighted blanket or your favorite stuffed animal, your nervous system gets a subtle but clear message: “You’re okay here.” This reassures the brain and calms the spirit, creating fertile ground for breakthroughs, emotional releases, and healing.

My own breakthrough moments - the ones that reshaped my understanding of trauma and connection - came when I was wrapped tightly in a weighted blanket, feeling the gentle pressure like a soft embrace. Those moments revealed how physical comfort reaches parts of the brain that words can’t touch, especially when we’re peeling back the layers of self with psychedelic medicines.

This article is an invitation to explore how blankets and comfort objects quietly hold space during psychedelic sessions. Why they work, what science says, and how you can make the most of these tactile companions. It’s about reclaiming the humble blanket from something you “might want” to something you “need” to guide your journey inward.

2. Core Concept Explanation with Scientific Backing

The idea that physical touch and pressure can soothe our nervous system is ancient and deeply wired into our biology. When we talk about blankets and comfort objects during psychedelic sessions, we’re really talking about sensory grounding - using the body’s connection to touch to regulate the mind. This connection is rooted in the way our nervous system processes gentle pressure.

Weighted blankets, for example, apply deep pressure stimulation (DPS), which research shows can promote the release of serotonin and melatonin - chemicals that help regulate mood and induce calmness. It’s a simple but powerful mechanism. The pressure mimics the sensation of a hug or being held, calming the sympathetic nervous system (which triggers fight-or-flight) and activating the parasympathetic nervous system (responsible for relaxation and rest).

Neuroscientist Robin Carhart-Harris has shown through his work at Imperial College London that psychedelics reduce activity in the brain’s default mode network (DMN), the area involved in self-referential thoughts and ego maintenance. When the DMN quiets down, our usual psychological defenses fall away, leaving us vulnerable but also open to healing. Physical touch and comfort from objects help anchor stable sensations during this fragile state.

There’s also an embodied cognition angle: our brain interprets physical sensations as signals about safety and emotional state. When your body feels safe through touch - whether it’s the weight of a blanket or the softness of a familiar object - your brain’s fear response is less likely to hijack the experience. This is why trauma workers like Bessel van der Kolk emphasize physical safety and sensation in healing, as outlined in The Body Keeps the Score. Psychedelic sessions often unearth old wounds, and having a comfort object or blanket can prevent retraumatization by easing the nervous system into a calmer state.

Comfort objects can also serve as a kind of “anchor” for the mind - a focus point that helps prevent overwhelming dissociation or anxiety. Stanislav Grof, a pioneer in psychedelic therapy, noted that grounding techniques are essential to move through intense sessions. The tactile reassurance from a blanket or a familiar object can be a lifeline when the mind wanders too far or the emotions surge unexpectedly.

Ultimately, the science says this: comfort through touch shapes our brain chemistry and nervous system responses. During a psychedelic session, when the mind is exposed and raw, blankets and comfort objects provide a low-key but deep way to maintain safety and presence. They don’t just keep you warm. They keep you steady.

3. Research and Evidence - Studies Illuminating Touch and Psychedelic Healing

While research directly combining blankets or comfort objects with psychedelic therapy is limited, there’s a strong foundation of related scientific findings that shed light on why these items matter. We can start by looking at studies on weighted blankets to understand how deep pressure affects the nervous system.

A 2015 study by Dr. Lisa A. Kiel and colleagues at the University of Delaware investigated the effects of weighted blankets on adults with anxiety. Participants reported significant reductions in anxiety and improved relaxation when using weighted blankets. The study’s findings support the idea that deep pressure stimulation can reduce physiological arousal by increasing parasympathetic nervous system activity, which encourages calm states.

Then there’s the body of research on sensory grounding and trauma therapy. Peter Levine, known for his work on somatic experiencing, emphasizes that trauma is stored not just in memory but in the body’s nervous system. Touch and physical comfort help “remap” traumatic stress responses by signaling safety to the brain. This is echoed in studies showing that tactile stimulation can lower cortisol levels - the body’s primary stress hormone - bringing the nervous system back into balance.

psychedelic research, Griffiths and Carhart-Harris have published numerous studies showcasing the importance of “set and setting” for safe and effective psychedelic experiences. While their research focuses more on external environment and psychological mindset, it consistently points to the need for physical comfort and safety. This includes soft lighting, supportive guides, and yes - comfort objects and blankets. These elements create an environment that supports the brain’s natural healing processes.

Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind explores how context and comfort during sessions influence the quality of psychedelic breakthroughs, hinting at why something as simple as a blanket can be life-changing. When the body is warm and weighted, psychedelic experiences are less likely to be derailed by physical discomfort or anxiety spikes.

Additionally, the psychological comfort offered by familiar objects is supported by attachment theory. Research on attachment and transitional objects shows that holding or touching items connected to safety figures or personal memories reduces distress and promotes emotional regulation, especially in times of stress. This means that bringing a favorite blanket or personal item into a session can have subconscious healing effects beyond physical warmth.

While these studies don’t yet directly link blankets or comfort objects to psychedelic outcomes, the evidence from anxiety research, trauma therapy, attachment theory, and psychedelic set and setting research converges on one point: tactile comfort is a powerful, evidence-backed ally in navigating vulnerable inner states.

4. Practical Application - How to Bring Comfort Objects into Your Sessions

If you’re planning a psychedelic session - whether guided or solo - consider this your permission slip to bring a blanket and at least one comfort object. These aren’t just “nice to have” extras; they’re part of creating a safe container for your experience.

Start by choosing a blanket that feels good to you. Many people prefer weighted blankets because of their calming deep pressure effect. If you’re interested, check out this Weighted Blanket for Anxiety. The weight should generally be around 10% of your body weight, but comfort is the ultimate guide. Don’t force a blanket that feels restrictive or hot; it should be cozy and grounding, not suffocating.

Bring the blanket into your preparation ritual. Before your session starts, wrap yourself in it while setting intentions or meditating. This pre-session connection helps your brain associate the blanket with safety and calm. During the session, keep it nearby or draped over your body, especially during the peak when emotions and sensations intensify.

Comfort objects can be anything meaningful to you - a stuffed animal, a smooth stone, a piece of fabric, or even a scented cloth. The key is familiarity and emotional resonance. For some, a family heirloom or a small keepsake evokes safety; for others, a plush toy or soft shawl works best. Have it within easy reach so you can instinctively grab it whenever you feel the need to ground.

It’s also worth discussing comfort objects with your guide or sitter beforehand. They can help remind you to use these tools when the session gets challenging. Sometimes, simply holding the blanket or stroking a soft object can interrupt spiraling thoughts or anxiety.

As with all psychedelic preparation, test your comfort objects in non-psychedelic moments. Use your blanket during meditation or quiet evenings to build that soothing association. This way, when you’re under the influence, the touch feels familiar and reassuring instead of foreign or distracting.

Don’t underestimate the power of simple things, especially when the mind is vulnerable. Bringing a blanket and a comfort object means bringing a physical kind of kindness to your journey. It’s a way of telling your nervous system, “You are taken care of.”

5. Common Challenges and How to Work Through Them

Even with the best intentions, blankets and comfort objects can sometimes bring up unexpected challenges during psychedelic sessions. It’s not always smooth sailing. Knowing what might come up and how to handle it can make your experience less frustrating and more fluid.

One common challenge is that a blanket or comfort object can unexpectedly trigger difficult memories or emotions. This happens because these items are often tied to childhood or past experiences, which the psychedelic mind can unearth with high sensitivity. What was once comforting can become a doorway to old wounds.

If this happens, resist the impulse to reject the object outright. Instead, sit with the discomfort and remind yourself that the blanket or object is a tool, not the source of distress. Sometimes, naming the feeling aloud or journaling about it after the session helps untangle emotions. Remember what Bessel van der Kolk teaches in The Body Keeps the Score: that trauma stored in the body can get reactivated through sensation, but gentle attention and compassion can allow healing to happen.

Another challenge is discomfort or overheating. Some people find weighted blankets too restrictive or hot, especially during an intense trip when the body’s temperature regulation is altered. If this is the case, try a lighter blanket or a soft shawl instead. Layering can help you adjust according to your body’s signals. Always listen to your body’s feedback.

For those who feel overly attached to their comfort objects, there’s a risk of clinging too hard, which can block the session’s fluidity. Psychedelics encourage surrender and openness, and sometimes gripping a blanket or toy too tightly can actually increase anxiety. If you notice this, try to consciously loosen your grip or place the object gently beside you. Practice mindful breathing to shift your focus from the object to inner sensations.

Finally, sometimes guides or sitters may underestimate the power of comfort objects and not offer blankets or space for them. Advocating for your own needs is crucial. Explain your preferences before the session and make sure your physical and emotional safety needs are honored. A good sitter understands that these tactile items are part of your psychological toolkit, not frivolous extras.

Working through these challenges means embracing imperfection and curiosity. Every session is a learning experience. Blankets and comfort objects aren’t magic; they’re companions that sometimes need to be adjusted or redefined as your relationship with healing evolves.

6. Integration with Daily Life - Long-Term Perspective

Many people think of blankets and comfort objects only in the context of a psychedelic trip, but their true power extends far beyond that single moment. After the session ends and the mushrooms wear off, how you engage with these tactile tools can influence your ongoing healing and integration.

Integration, as James Fadiman emphasizes, is where the real work begins. Bringing insights from the session into daily life requires stability and groundedness. This is why I often recommend continuing to use your comfort objects and blankets after the trip. They serve as gentle reminders of safety and self-care when old patterns or anxieties resurface.

For example, wrapping yourself in that same weighted blanket after a challenging day can reconnect you to the calm you experienced during your session. It’s a way of “closing the circle” on your healing journey, linking psychedelic insights with physical and emotional comfort. This practice bridges the gap between mind and body, an integration strategy that Bessel van der Kolk highlights as essential in trauma recovery.

Comfort objects can also become part of mindfulness or meditation practices, deepening your relationship to embodied presence. Holding a soft cloth or stone during quiet reflection helps cultivate awareness of the present moment and your body’s sensations without judgment. This ongoing engagement builds resilience, so future sessions or life stresses don’t feel as overwhelming.

On a broader scale, nurturing this relationship with tactile comfort fosters self-compassion. The simple act of caring for your physical needs mirrors the internal care you learn through psychedelic therapy. It’s a daily act of kindness that supports lasting mental health.

Moreover, bringing these tools into your everyday environment - your home, your bedroom, your sanctuary - creates a warm, inviting space where you can rest and recover. The presence of blankets and comfort objects resets your nervous system repeatedly, something that’s often undervalued in a world that prizes performance over rest.

Integrating these tactile companions into daily life helps translate psychedelic healing into sustainable well-being. It’s not just about the trip; it’s about the long road of reclaiming your sense of safety and love, moment by moment.

7. Closing Reflection - A New Thought to Sit With

Here’s a thought I want to leave you with: what if the most deep part of your psychedelic journey isn’t just the visions or revelations, but the simple, quiet comfort of feeling held? In a world full of noise, chaos, and disconnection, the act of wrapping yourself in a blanket or clutching a familiar object becomes an act of radical self-kindness.

Blankets and comfort objects are humble, tactile bookmarks in the story of our healing. They gently remind us that no matter how far we travel into altered states or untangle the knots of past trauma, we always come home - to our bodies, to this moment, to ourselves. This homecoming is as sacred as any mystical insight.

Stanislav Grof spoke about the “perinatal matrices” and how early experiences of safety or trauma deeply influence our psyche. What if, through blankets and comfort, we create new sensory memories - ones of nurturing and calm - that rewrite the nervous system’s story? These small, physical acts carry enormous emotional weight.

I encourage you to hold that image in your mind next time you prepare for a session or find yourself overwhelmed in daily life. The power of touch is ancient, elemental, and often overlooked. It’s not about fancy rituals or complex practices; it’s simply about being held - whether by the arms of another, the weight of a blanket, or the softness of a cherished object.

In the end, psychedelic healing is as much about rediscovering our capacity for tenderness as it is about insight. So when you prepare for your next session, don’t forget that a blanket or comfort object might just be your most faithful companion on the journey within.
